did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used a multimodal deep learning framework integrating three CNN architectures (DenseNet-121, EfficientNet-B3, ResNet-50) with patient metadata to classify skin lesions from the HAM10000 dataset. It also employed XRAI (eXplanation with Region-based Attribution for Images) for explainability in dermatological diagnosis.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the implementation and validation of a multimodal deep learning framework using the HAM10000 dataset. It reports specific performance metrics (e.g., 89.09% test accuracy for EfficientNet-B3), compares three CNN architectures, implements XRAI explainability, and validates a web-based clinical interface, which constitutes a technical study producing new results rather than a literature review.

title: Web-Based Multimodal Deep Learning Platform with XRAI Explainability for Real-Time Skin Lesion Classification and Clinical Decision Support
brief_summary: This study developed a web-based multimodal deep learning platform for real-time skin lesion classification to address the need for accessible and explainable diagnostic tools. The framework integrated three CNN architectures (DenseNet-121, EfficientNet-B3, ResNet-50) with patient metadata using the HAM10000 dataset of 10,015 dermatoscopic images. EfficientNet-B3 achieved the highest performance with 89.09% test accuracy, outperforming the other models. The system incorporated XRAI explainability to provide spatially coherent attention patterns aligned with clinical reasoning for conditions like melanoma and basal cell carcinoma. A deployable web interface was validated for real-time inference, offering risk stratification and cosmetic guidance for benign conditions. The research demonstrates that combining high diagnostic accuracy with explainable AI can bridge the gap between research innovation and practical clinical utility.

lesion_types_studied.details: Most prevalent class in HAM10000 dataset (66.9%, 6705 images). Classified with high precision (0.939) and recall (0.956) by EfficientNet-B3.
Critical malignant condition (11.1%, 1113 images). EfficientNet-B3 achieved 81.6% confidence in web interface validation and F1 of 0.703 in per-class analysis.
Benign condition (11.0%, 1099 images). EfficientNet-B3 achieved F1 of 0.781. Distinguished from actinic keratoses via surface texture analysis.
Malignant condition (5.1%, 514 images). EfficientNet-B3 achieved 82.1% confidence in web interface validation and F1 of 0.853 in per-class analysis.
Precancerous condition (3.3%, 327 images). EfficientNet-B3 achieved 88% confidence in web interface validation and F1 of 0.792 in per-class analysis.
Benign condition (1.4%, 142 images). EfficientNet-B3 achieved high performance (F1 0.930) and 97.0% confidence in web interface validation.
Rare benign condition (1.1%, 115 images). EfficientNet-B3 achieved F1 of 0.741 and 98.0% confidence in web interface validation.

all_task_types.target: Seven diagnostic categories of skin lesions (melanocytic nevi, melanoma, benign keratosis-like lesions, basal cell carcinoma, actinic keratoses, vascular lesions, dermatofibroma)
all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly focuses on 'skin lesion classification' using the HAM10000 dataset. The model predicts discrete diagnostic categories (multiclass classification with 7 classes). Performance is measured using accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score, and confidence probabilities for class prediction. The study compares DenseNet-121, EfficientNet-B3, and ResNet-50 architectures for this classification task.

datasets_used.name: HAM10000 (Human Against Machine with 10,000 training images)
datasets_used.description: A comprehensive collection of 10,015 high-resolution dermatoscopic images collected from diverse populations using multiple acquisition modalities and imaging devices. Originally developed for the International Skin Imaging Collaboration (ISIC) 2018 challenge, it includes patient metadata (age, sex, anatomical location) and ground truth labels established via histopathological confirmation (>50%), expert consensus, follow-up, or confocal microscopy.

use.training: Used for training three CNN architectures (DenseNet-121, EfficientNet-B3, ResNet-50) with multimodal input (images + metadata). Partitioned into 70% for training.
use.validation: Used for model validation during training. Partitioned into 15% for validation.
use.testing: Used for final model evaluation and web application validation. Partitioned into 15% for testing. Seven representative test images (one per class) were selected from this set for web interface validation.
use.other: Used for preprocessing normalization parameter calculation (stratified sampling of 1996 images) and XRAI explainability analysis.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To compare the performance of different CNN architectures (DenseNet-121, EfficientNet-B3, ResNet-50) and establish statistical significance of performance differences.
To calculate effect sizes for pairwise comparisons between models, indicating the magnitude of practical improvements.
To confirm the reliability of performance advantages and ensure intervals exclude zero, validating that differences are not due to chance.
To quantify the similarity or divergence between model attention patterns (attributions) for predicted versus true classes during error analysis.

hypothesis_testing.null_hypothesis: There is no significant difference in performance (accuracy) between the compared CNN architectures (EfficientNet-B3 vs. DenseNet-121, EfficientNet-B3 vs. ResNet-50, and DenseNet-121 vs. ResNet-50).
hypothesis_testing.alternative_hypothesis: There is a significant difference in performance (accuracy) between the compared CNN architectures.
hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The study reported p-values less than 0.05 for all pairwise comparisons (p=0.011, p=0.002, p=0.010 for overall accuracy; p=0.009, p=0.002 for per-class F1-scores), indicating that the performance differences were statistically significant. The null hypothesis was rejected in favor of the alternative, confirming that EfficientNet-B3 demonstrated statistically superior performance compared to the other architectures.

HAM10000.notes: EfficientNet-B3 demonstrated statistically superior performance compared to DenseNet-121 and ResNet-50 (p < 0.05 for all comparisons). TTA improved EfficientNet-B3 accuracy by 1.00 percentage point. XRAI analysis showed focus ratios between 0.103 and 0.132 across classes.

architecture_used.main_model: Multimodal Deep Learning Framework integrating three CNN architectures: DenseNet-121, EfficientNet-B3, and ResNet-50.
architecture_used.pretraining: ImageNet pre-trained weights for the EfficientNet-B3 backbone; DenseNet-121 and ResNet-50 details not explicitly specified as pre-trained in the provided text, but standard practice implied.
architecture_used.input_size: 224 x 224 pixels (images); Metadata includes age (normalized 0-1), sex (one-hot), and anatomical location (one-hot, 12 regions).

classification_or_output_head.description: Fusion of image-derived feature vectors and patient metadata (age, sex, anatomical location) into a joint feature vector, followed by a classification head. Identical classification head architecture used across all models for fair comparison.

metadata_encoding.anatomical_location: One-hot encoding (12 regions: abdomen, back, chest, face, foot, hand, lower extremity, neck, scalp, trunk, upper extremity)

preprocessing_techniques: Resizing to 224 × 224 pixels using high-quality interpolation algorithms
Custom channel-wise normalization using dataset-specific statistics (means: [0.763, 0.544, 0.568]; standard deviations: [0.141, 0.152, 0.169]) derived from stratified sampling of 1996 images
Age normalization using z-score standardization with imputation of missing values with population mean
Gender encoding using binary representation (male = 1, female = 0)
Anatomical location encoding using one-hot encoding for twelve body regions
Stratified random sampling for dataset partitioning to maintain proportional class representation
Test-time augmentation (TTA) involving eight transformations: original image, horizontal flip, vertical flip, ±15-degree rotation, top-left crop, bottom-right crop, and 90% scaling

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Applied during inference on test samples using eight distinct transformations: original image, horizontal flip, vertical flip, +15-degree rotation, -15-degree rotation, top-left crop, bottom-right crop, and 90% scaling. Predictions from all eight augmented versions were ensemble-averaged to produce the final classification result. Metadata was kept consistent across all augmented image versions.

conclusions: EfficientNet-B3 emerged as the superior architecture for skin lesion classification, achieving 89.09% test accuracy and 90.08% validation accuracy, significantly outperforming DenseNet-121 (82.83%) and ResNet-50 (78.78%) with large effect sizes (Cohen’s d > 0.8) and statistical significance (p < 0.05).

The integration of patient metadata (age, sex, anatomical location) with dermatoscopic images in a multimodal framework enhanced diagnostic performance, with EfficientNet-B3 demonstrating the best balance of sensitivity and specificity across seven diagnostic categories.

Test-time augmentation (TTA) provided a reliable performance boost across all architectures, with EfficientNet-B3 showing the most pronounced improvement (+1.00 percentage point, reaching 90.09% accuracy) without requiring model retraining.

The first implementation of XRAI (eXplanation with Region-based Attribution for Images) provided spatially coherent explanations aligned with clinical reasoning, highlighting irregular pigmentation for melanoma, ulcerated borders for basal cell carcinoma, and surface irregularities for precancerous lesions.

EfficientNet-B3 demonstrated a safer diagnostic error profile by minimizing clinically dangerous misclassifications (e.g., classifying malignant melanoma as benign), whereas ResNet-50 exhibited severe deficiencies in detecting minority and high-risk classes like melanoma and dermatofibroma.

The developed web-based clinical interface successfully validated real-time diagnostic capabilities, offering risk stratification, emergency protocols for malignant conditions, and cosmetic guidance for benign lesions, bridging the gap between research-grade AI and practical clinical utility.

The study establishes the readiness of the multimodal deep learning platform for controlled clinical trials and potential integration into healthcare workflows, particularly to benefit underserved regions with limited specialist availability.
important_remarks: The study acknowledges potential data leakage because the HAM10000 dataset contains multiple images per lesion, and the stratified random splitting used may allow images from the same lesion to appear across training, validation, and test sets.

The HAM10000 dataset is dominated by benign nevi (66.9%) and primarily represents lighter Fitzpatrick skin phototypes and Central European populations, limiting generalizability to darker skin tones and diverse ethnic groups.
The HAM10000 dataset does not include invasive squamous cell carcinoma (SCC), only actinic keratoses/intraepithelial carcinoma, leaving a gap in coverage for major skin cancer types.

The authors state that formal significance testing procedures (e.g., paired t-tests, confidence intervals, bootstrap resampling) were not applied in the present analysis, despite reporting statistical comparisons in other sections; they recommend future work incorporate these for rigorous validation.

Oversampling strategies like SMOTE were not applied to address class imbalance to preserve dataset authenticity, which limited sensitivity in underrepresented categories such as dermatofibroma and melanoma.

The study did not explicitly measure the incremental contribution of patient metadata compared to image-only models, nor did it analyze subgroup performance stratified by demographic factors or evaluate robustness to missing/erroneous metadata.
Metrics such as AUROC, PR-AUC, and per-class sensitivity/specificity with confidence intervals were not included in the analysis, focusing instead on accuracy and F1-scores.

The model's performance is limited on cases with indistinct lesion boundaries and atypical morphologies, which often led to misclassification despite strong alignment between model attention and clinically relevant regions.
